</div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Social&#x20;identity&#x20;theory&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[theory]</span> says that the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>social group</u> we affiliate with</span>&nbsp;can <i>influence</i> the way we perceive <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">other people in our in-group</span> and those from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">other out-groups</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Social identity theory</b></span> says that the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>social group</u> we affiliate with</span>&nbsp;can <i>influence</i> the way we perceive <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">other people in our in-group</span> and those from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">other out-groups</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: going to the University of Alabama might make us like other people from Alabama, while making us dislike people from Auburn.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-a310319f61c2db6a0dd37eff9a5d9bf43276d934.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Social identity theory</b></span> says that the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>social group</u> we affiliate with</span>&nbsp;can <i>influence</i> the way we perceive <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""other&#x20;people&#x20;in&#x20;our&#x20;in&#x2D;group"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> and those from <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""other&#x20;out&#x2D;groups"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Social identity theory</b></span> says that the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>social group</u> we affiliate with</span>&nbsp;can <i>influence</i> the way we perceive <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">other people in our in-group</span> and those from <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">other out-groups</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: going to the University of Alabama might make us like other people from Alabama, while making us dislike people from Auburn.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-a310319f61c2db6a0dd37eff9a5d9bf43276d934.jpg""></div>"
"<div>According to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;social&#x20;identity&#x20;theory&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[theory]</span>, we're more likely to <i>like</i>&nbsp;people in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">our in-groups</span> than we are people from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">different out-groups</span>.</div>"	"<div>According to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>social identity theory</b></span>, we're more likely to <i>like</i>&nbsp;people in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">our in-groups</span> than we are people from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">different out-groups</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Theory that describes how the social groups we're in might <u>influence our perceptions</u> of people from either the same in-group or people from different out-groups.</div><div>- Ex: going to the University of Alabama might make us like other people from Alabama, while making us dislike people from Auburn.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-a310319f61c2db6a0dd37eff9a5d9bf43276d934.jpg""></div>"
"<div>According to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>social identity theory</b></span>, we're more likely to <i>like</i>&nbsp;people in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""our&#x20;in&#x2D;groups"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> than we are people from <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""different&#x20;out&#x2D;groups"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>According to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>social identity theory</b></span>, we're more likely to <i>like</i>&nbsp;people in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">our in-groups</span> than we are people from <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">different out-groups</span>.</div>

"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Biological preparedness</b></span> says it's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;much&#x20;easier&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[easier/harder]</span>&nbsp;to form associations between taste → nausea than taste → an electric shock.<br></div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Biological preparedness</b></span> says it's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>much easier</i></span>&nbsp;to form associations between taste → nausea than taste → an electric shock.<br></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Social identity theory</b></span> says that the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>social group</u> we affiliate with</span>&nbsp;can <i>influence</i> the way we perceive <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">other people in our in-group</span> and those from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">other out-groups</span>.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Desensitization</b></span> occurs when you slowly get used to a stimulus over time after&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;previously&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;being&#x20;&lt;u&gt;sensitized&#x20;to&#x20;it&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Desensitization</b></span> occurs when you slowly get used to a stimulus over time after&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>previously&nbsp;</i>being <u>sensitized to it</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Desensitization only applies when you were previously ""sensitized"" to a stimulus. If you weren't previously sensitized to it, you can't get desensitized to it.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7e1743ab786b03f5e6e58ec8ca9668e4beb276ef.jpg""></div>"
"The concept of the <b>sick role</b> was developed by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Talcott&#x20;Parsons"" data-ordinal=""1"">[person]</span>."	"The concept of the <b>sick role</b> was developed by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Talcott Parsons</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Talcott Parsons was also resposnible for developing <b>functionalism</b> (along with <u>Emile Dunkenheim</u>).</div><div>- If the individual doesn't return to work, they're labeled as deviant.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-37ee49d2d9402a124fd39318e6d115349f8ae3f7.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""sick-person.jpg""></div>"
"<div>The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>sick role</b></span> in society is a form of deviance, where individuals are expected to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;want&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;to&#x20;get&#x20;better&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> while also being obligated to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;return&#x20;to&#x20;work&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> once they're healthy.</div>"	"<div>The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>sick role</b></span> in society is a form of deviance, where individuals are expected to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>want</i> to get better</u></span> while also being obligated to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>return to work</u></span> once they're healthy.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Developed by Talcott Parsons.</div><div>- While they're sick, they're allowed to be exempt from their normal duties.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-37ee49d2d9402a124fd39318e6d115349f8ae3f7.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""sick-person.jpg""></div>"

"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Role tension</b></span> is a <i>general</i> term that encompasses <u>role strain</u>, <u>role conflict</u>, and <u>role exit</u>.

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Incentive&#x20;theory&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[theory]</span> focuses on how our behaviors are <i>motivated</i> by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>external</i> factors (rewards, community values, etc.)</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Incentive theory</b></span> focuses on how our behaviors are <i>motivated</i> by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>external</i> factors (rewards, community values, etc.)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Ex: we want <u>money</u>, so we're incentivized to work.</div><div>- This theory does not apply to internal rewards, like feeling a sense of accomplishment.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-4a14c5beadb37f5c4d4301d5cc8cf2bd8f6f2f31.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Incentive theory</b></span> focuses on how our behaviors are <i>motivated</i> by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;external&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;factors&#x20;&#x28;rewards&#x2C;&#x20;community&#x20;values&#x2C;&#x20;etc&#x2E;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Incentive theory</b></span> focuses on how our behaviors are <i>motivated</i> by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>external</i> factors (rewards, community values, etc.)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Ex: we want <u>money</u>, so we're incentivized to work.</div><div>- This theory does not apply to internal rewards, like feeling a sense of accomplishment.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-4a14c5beadb37f5c4d4301d5cc8cf2bd8f6f2f31.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Expectancy&#x20;violation&#x20;theory&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[theory]</span> is a theory of communication that analyzes <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">how people <i>respond</i></span>&nbsp;when their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">expectations of a situation <u>turn out wrong</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Expectancy violation theory</b></span> is a theory of communication that analyzes <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">how people <i>respond</i></span>&nbsp;when their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">expectations of a situation <u>turn out wrong</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: a person wasn't expecting to get pulled over late at night when they thought they could drive around at whatever speed they wanted.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-5da02ef3d96d64369f04777e91cdac15e93a14ad.jpg""></div>"

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Positive&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> <b>symtoms</b> of any mental disorder are behaviors <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>present</i></span> in the <u>individual</u> but <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>absent</i></span> from the <u>rest of the population</u>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Positive</b></span> <b>symtoms</b> of any mental disorder are behaviors <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>present</i></span> in the <u>individual</u> but <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>absent</i></span> from the <u>rest of the population</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Positive/negative symptom terminology can be applied to any mental disorder, not just schizophrenia.</div><div>- Think of the positive/negative symptoms of <u>schizophrenia</u> for example.</div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Positive</b></span> <b>symtoms</b> of any mental disorder are behaviors <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;present&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[absent/present]</span> in the <u>individual</u> but <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;absent&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[absent/present]</span> from the <u>rest of the population</u>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Positive</b></span> <b>symtoms</b> of any mental disorder are behaviors <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>present</i></span> in the <u>individual</u> but <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>absent</i></span> from the <u>rest of the population</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Positive/negative symptom terminology can be applied to any mental disorder, not just schizophrenia.</div><div>- Think of the positive/negative symptoms of <u>schizophrenia</u> for example.</div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Negative&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> <b>symptoms</b> of any mental disorder are behaviors <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>absent</i></span> in the <u>individual</u> but <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>present</i></span> in the <u>rest of the population</u>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Negative</b></span> <b>symptoms</b> of any mental disorder are behaviors <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>absent</i></span> in the <u>individual</u> but <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>present</i></span> in the <u>rest of the population</u>.

"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>base rate fallacy</b></span> is when people tend to place higher importance on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>newly</i> presented information</u></span> that applies to <i>one specific</i> scenario instead of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>generally</i> accepted information</span>.

<div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YuURK_q2NR8"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YuURK_q2NR8</a><br></div><div>- Type of <b>representativeness heuristic</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-dffb38f56f0532564137b621e7b6bd777d208e1f.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-26502456eac0f4998043eb794d588b105ffcc156.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Gardner&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[person]</span><b>'s</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;theory&#x20;of&#x20;multiple&#x20;intelligences&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[theory]</span> believes our <u>intelligence</u> can take on 8 different forms: musical, visual-spatial, verbal, logical, kinesthetic, interpersonal, intrapersonal, naturalistic."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Gardner</b></span><b>'s</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>theory of multiple intelligences</b></span> believes our <u>intelligence</u> can take on 8 different forms: musical, visual-spatial, verbal, logical, kinesthetic, interpersonal, intrapersonal, naturalistic.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"According to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;opponent&#x2D;process&#x20;theory&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[theory]</span> of drug usage, <i>continued</i> drug use leads to increased tolerance &amp; decreased pleasure."	"According to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>opponent-process theory</b></span> of drug usage, <i>continued</i> drug use leads to increased tolerance &amp; decreased pleasure.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Opponent-process theory of <u>drug usage</u> &amp; opponent-process theory of <u>color vision</u> are two separate theories.</div><div><br></div><img src=""Screen Shot 2019-03-19 at 8.54.54 PM.png""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;functional&#x20;attitudes&#x20;theory&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[theory]</span> says that our <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;attitudes&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> towards things serve four functions:<div><br></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Knowledge</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Adaptive</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Ego-expressive</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Ego-defensive</span></div>"	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>functional attitudes theory</b></span> says that our <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>attitudes</u></span> towards things serve four functions:<div><br></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Knowledge</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Adaptive</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Ego-expressive</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Ego-defensive</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>Knowledge</b>: we can predict other people's behavior if we know their attitudes.<div>- <b>Adaptive</b>: we'll be accpeted by society if we express attitudes that are socially acceptable.</div><div>- <b>Ego-expressive</b>: our attitudes can help us express our self-identity (wearing your favorite team's jersey).</div><div>- <b>Ego-defensive</b>: we can use our attitude toward something to justify actions we know are wrong.</div></div>"

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Change&#x20;theory&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[theory]</span>&nbsp;describes the 6 different stages a person goes through when they <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>first</i> <u>modify their behavior</u></span>...<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>Pre-contemplation</b></span>: point in time when individuals are not even considering changing.<div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>Contemplation</b></span>: point in time where individuals consider that they may need to change.</div><div>3. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>Determination</b></span>: point where they make a serious change to stop their behavior in the future.</div><div>4. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><b>Action</b></span>: point where individuals put their plan into action</div><div>5. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><b>Maintenance</b></span>: point when the individual has less of a feeling of threat of returning to old patterns.</div><div>6. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><b>Termination</b></span>: point where individuals no longer find the new behavior to be a threat.</div></div>"	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Change theory</b></span>&nbsp;describes the 6 different stages a person goes through when they <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>first</i> <u>modify their behavior</u></span>...<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>Pre-contemplation</b></span>: point in time when individuals are not even considering changing.<div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>Contemplation</b></span>: point in time where individuals consider that they may need to change.</div><div>3. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>Determination</b></span>: point where they make a serious change to stop their behavior in the future.</div><div>4. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><b>Action</b></span>: point where individuals put their plan into action</div><div>5. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><b>Maintenance</b></span>: point when the individual has less of a feeling of threat of returning to old patterns.</div><div>6. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><b>Termination</b></span>: point where individuals no longer find the new behavior to be a threat.</div></div>

</div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Change theory</b></span>&nbsp;describes the 6 different stages a person goes through when they <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;first&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;modify&#x20;their&#x20;behavior&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>...<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>Contemplation</b></span>: point in time where individuals consider that they may need to change.</div><div>3. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" "<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Carl Jung</u></span> is the psychologist responsible for identifying the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">different <b>archetypes</b></span> of our personality that stem from the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">unconscious</span> mind.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Personal unconscious</b>: total amount of information in a person's mind, but it's not always something that can be recalled.</div><div>- <b>Collective unconscious</b>: residues of our early ancestors that all humans have in the back of their minds.</div><div>- <b>Persona</b>: the portion of our personality we show to the world.</div><div>- <b>Anima</b>: males acting femanine.</div><div>- <b>Animus</b>: females acting masculine.</div><div>- <b>Shadow</b>: unpleasant thoughts in our consciousness.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e352adfd094bb1e44f5d21e20f1f4a83e1e66e4a.jpg""></div>"

<div>- <b>Personal unconscious</b>: total amount of information in a person's mind, but it's not always something that can be recalled.</div><div>- <b>Collective unconscious</b>: residues of our early ancestors that all humans have in the back of their minds.</div><div>- <b>Persona</b>: the portion of our personality we show to the world.</div><div>- <b>Anima</b>: males acting femanine.</div><div>- <b>Animus</b>: females acting masculine.</div><div>- <b>Shadow</b>: unpleasant thoughts in our consciousness.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e352adfd094bb1e44f5d21e20f1f4a83e1e66e4a.jpg""></div>"

"In evolutionary game theory, a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Nash equilibrium</b></span> is when people make choices that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;maximize&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;their&#x20;self&#x2D;interest&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - <i>regardless</i> of what other people choose to do."	"In evolutionary game theory, a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Nash equilibrium</b></span> is when people make choices that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>maximize</i> their self-interest</u></span> - <i>regardless</i> of what other people choose to do.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Similarity bias</b></span>&nbsp;is how we tend to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>favor</i> people <u>similar</u> to us</span>&nbsp;moreso than people different from us.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Cognitive behavioral therapy</b></span> can be used to help people with things like insomnia or smoking addictions because it can help&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>change&nbsp;</i><u>negative patterns</u> of behavior</span>.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Carl Jung</u></span> is the psychologist responsible for identifying the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">different <b>archetypes</b></span> of our personality that stem from the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""unconscious"" data-ordinal=""3"">[conscious/unconscious]</span> mind."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Carl Jung</u></span> is the psychologist responsible for identifying the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">different <b>archetypes</b></span> of our personality that stem from the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">unconscious</span> mind.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Personal unconscious</b>: total amount of information in a person's mind, but it's not always something that can be recalled.</div><div>- <b>Collective unconscious</b>: residues of our early ancestors that all humans have in the back of their minds.</div><div>- <b>Persona</b>: the portion of our personality we show to the world.</div><div>- <b>Anima</b>: males acting femanine.</div><div>- <b>Animus</b>: females acting masculine.</div><div>- <b>Shadow</b>: unpleasant thoughts in our consciousness.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e352adfd094bb1e44f5d21e20f1f4a83e1e66e4a.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;Neuroleptic&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;medications"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;perscribed to <b>schizophrenic</b> individuals aim to <i>reduce</i> their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">positive</span> symptoms - inadvertently <i>increasing</i> their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">negative</span> symptoms."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Neuroleptic</u> medications</span>&nbsp;perscribed to <b>schizophrenic</b> individuals aim to <i>reduce</i> their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">positive</span> symptoms - inadvertently <i>increasing</i> their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">negative</span> symptoms.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>These depress the nervous system, so they can actually make negative symptoms worse as a side-effect.</div><div><br></div><img src=""what-are-the-symptoms-of-schizophrenia-2953120-cba74c5e1dd942ecafde1824217603f9.png""></div>"

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Altercasting</b></span> is a type of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">impression management</span> technique where you <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>project an identity <i>onto</i> someone else</u></span> that happens to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">align with <i>your</i> goals</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Salesman: ""since you two are both clearly health-conscious, you should be very interested in this probiotic.""</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;Apart of the&nbsp;<u>dramaturgical approach</u>&nbsp;outlined by&nbsp;<b>Erving Goffman</b>.</div><div><br></div><div>Impression management tehcniques...</div><div>- Self-disclosure: describing to other people what makes you unique.</div><div>- Managing appearances: trying to fit in.</div><div>- Altercasting: projecting an identity onto someone else to get them to agree with you.</div><div>- Aligning actions: make excuses for your questionable&nbsp;behavior to make it seem more acceptable to others.</div><div>- Ingration: winning people over through flattery or conformity.</div><div><br></div><img src=""392db704f3a56cdddc47a8a14bed6a42.png""><br></div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- Left superior temporal sulcus → recognizes anger.</div><div>- Insula &amp; basal ganglia → recognizes disgust.</div><div>- Subcallosal cingulate gyrus → recognizes sadness.</div></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b8f3745f1380741abbefe48eb817dadcf8293ff2.jpg""></div>"

"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Atypical antipsychotics (AAPs)</b></span> are drugs that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decrease</i></span> the levels of both <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>dopamine</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>serotonin</u></span> in psychotic schizophrenics - <i>reducing</i>&nbsp;side effects related to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">motor control</span>.

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Temporal&#x20;monotonicity&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> says that after a <u>painful experience</u>, adding <i>more</i> pain makes our retroactive perspective of the initial pain <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>worse</i></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Temporal monotonicity</b></span> says that after a <u>painful experience</u>, adding <i>more</i> pain makes our retroactive perspective of the initial pain <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>worse</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Adding even more pain → retroactive pain is worse.<div>- Adding pleasure → retroactive pain is better.</div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Temporal monotonicity</b></span> says that after a <u>painful experience</u>, adding <i>more</i> pain makes our retroactive perspective of the initial pain <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;worse&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[better/worse]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Temporal monotonicity</b></span> says that after a <u>painful experience</u>, adding <i>more</i> pain makes our retroactive perspective of the initial pain <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>worse</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Adding even more pain → retroactive pain is worse.<div>- Adding pleasure → retroactive pain is better.</div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Temporal&#x20;monotonicity&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> says that after a <u>painful experience</u>, adding <i>pleasure</i>&nbsp;makes our retroactive perspective of the initial pain <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>better</i></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Temporal monotonicity</b></span> says that after a <u>painful experience</u>, adding <i>pleasure</i>&nbsp;makes our retroactive perspective of the initial pain <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>better</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Adding even more pain → retroactive pain is worse.<div>- Adding pleasure → retroactive pain is better.</div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Temporal monotonicity</b></span> says that after a <u>painful experience</u>, adding <i>pleasure</i>&nbsp;makes our retroactive perspective of the initial pain <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;better&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[better/worse]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Temporal monotonicity</b></span> says that after a <u>painful experience</u>, adding <i>pleasure</i>&nbsp;makes our retroactive perspective of the initial pain <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>better</i></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Adding even more pain → retroactive pain is worse.<div>- Adding pleasure → retroactive pain is better.</div></div>"

</div>"

"An <i>accumulation</i> of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>acetylaldehyde</b></span> is associated with feelings of <u>nausea</u>, <u>headaches</u>, and <u>organ damage</u>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<div>In the <u>phosotransduction cascade</u>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>phosphodiesterase</b></span> catalyzes <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""cGMP"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""GMP"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>closing</i></span> Na<sup>+</sup> channels.</div>"	"<div>In the <u>phosotransduction cascade</u>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>phosphodiesterase</b></span> catalyzes <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">cGMP</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">GMP</span> - <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>closing</i></span> Na<sup>+</sup> channels.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Na<sup>+</sup> channels are held <u>open</u> by <b>cGMP</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""04b1e45463d5912ed6b62a1232fe0458.png""></div>"
"<div>In the <u>phosotransduction cascade</u>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>phosphodiesterase</b></span> catalyzes <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">cGMP</span> → <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">GMP</span> - <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;closing&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[opening/closing]</span> Na<sup>+</sup> channels.</div>"	"<div>In the <u>phosotransduction cascade</u>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>phosphodiesterase</b></span> catalyzes <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">cGMP</span> → <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">GMP</span> - <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>closing</i></span> Na<sup>+</sup> channels.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

""As a black man...""</div>"
"<div>Seeing this image as either <i>only</i> a pair of faces or <i>only</i> a vase resembles <b>Gestalt's law of</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""figure&#x2D;ground"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-fef866820f3873490fae303d2cf7999f85d75aae.jpg"">"	"<div>Seeing this image as either <i>only</i> a pair of faces or <i>only</i> a vase resembles <b>Gestalt's law of</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">figure-ground</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-fef866820f3873490fae303d2cf7999f85d75aae.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Figure-ground</b>: we see <u>only one part</u> of the image.</div>- <b>Multistability</b>: we can see the two images <u>interconvert</u>.</div>"
"<div>Seeing this image as <u>interconverting</u> between being&nbsp;a pair of faces and a vase resembles&nbsp;<b>Gestalt's law of</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""multistability"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-fef866820f3873490fae303d2cf7999f85d75aae.jpg"">"	"<div>Seeing this image as <u>interconverting</u> between being&nbsp;a pair of faces and a vase resembles&nbsp;<b>Gestalt's law of</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">multistability</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-fef866820f3873490fae303d2cf7999f85d75aae.jpg"">

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Figure-ground</b>: we see <u>only one part</u> of the image.</div>- <b>Multistability</b>: we can see the two images <u>interconvert</u>.</div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Visual&#x20;agnosia&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is a disorder where an individual can <i>see</i> but <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>cannot <i>interpret</i> visual information</u></span>, due to a defect in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">ventral</span> pathway of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">parietal</span> lobe."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Visual agnosia</b></span> is a disorder where an individual can <i>see</i> but <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>cannot <i>interpret</i> visual information</u></span>, due to a defect in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">ventral</span> pathway of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">parietal</span> lobe.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Recall that signals from the <b>parvocellular</b> pathway eventually travel to the anterior (ventral) portion of the cortex.<br></div><div>- Dorsal &amp; ventral pathways are apart of the <b>two-streams hypothesis</b>, which describes the pathways that control either <u>vision</u> or <u>audition</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e33de723066d9954d873e83b2a083a9b6da43093.jpg""></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Remember, the catecholamine theory sayss that depression is linked to the amount of serotonin &amp; norepinephrine in the synapse.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""Screen Shot 2019-04-10 at 3.23.26 AM.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Antidepressants</b></span> typically act as <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;agonists&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[agonists/antagonists]</span> for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;serotonin&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[neurotrans.]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;norepinephrine&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[neurotrans.]</span> in synapses."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Antidepressants</b></span> typically act as <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>agonists</i></span> for <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>serotonin</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>norepinephrine</u></span> in synapses.

"Difference between Karl Marx &amp; Max Weber in terms of their definitions of <u>social class</u>...<div><br></div><div>- Karl Marx: believed social class was defined by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;only&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;one&#x27;s&#x20;&lt;u&gt;income&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x28;&lt;i&gt;only&#x20;1&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;thing&#x29;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>- Max Weber: believed social class was defined by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;income&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x2C;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;social&#x20;status&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x2C;&#x20;and&#x20;&lt;u&gt;power&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x28;&lt;i&gt;multiple&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;things&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"Difference between Karl Marx &amp; Max Weber in terms of their definitions of <u>social class</u>...<div><br></div><div>- Karl Marx: believed social class was defined by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>only</i>&nbsp;one's <u>income</u>&nbsp;(<i>only 1</i> thing).</span></div><div>- Max Weber: believed social class was defined by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>income</u>, <u>social status</u>, and <u>power</u>&nbsp;(<i>multiple</i> things)</span>.</div>

"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">cognitive behavioral therapy</span>'s ABC model, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">'<b>C</b>'</span> stands for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;consequences&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;as&#x20;a&#x20;result&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;u&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;event&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">cognitive behavioral therapy</span>'s ABC model, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">'<b>C</b>'</span> stands for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i style="""">consequences</i>&nbsp;as a result of the <u style="""">event</u></span>.

"<div>In signal detection theory, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>conservative strategies</b></span> are when you <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;always&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;say&#x20;no&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> unless you're <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""100&#x25;&#x20;confident"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> the signal <i>is</i> there.</div>"	"<div>In signal detection theory, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>conservative strategies</b></span> are when you <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>always</i> <u>say no</u></span> unless you're <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">100% confident</span> the signal <i>is</i> there.</div>

Good thing → minimizes false positives<br><div>Bad thing → lots of misses.</div></div>"
"<div>In signal detection theory, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>liberal strategies</b></span> are when you <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;always&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;u&gt;say&#x20;yes&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, the signal <i>is</i> there.<br></div>"	"<div>In signal detection theory, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>liberal strategies</b></span> are when you <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>always</i>&nbsp;<u>say yes</u></span>, the signal <i>is</i> there.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Good thing → you will <i>always </i>detect its presence<br><div>Bad thing → lots of false positives.</div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Continuity theory</b></span> says people try to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;preserve&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;their&#x20;&lt;u&gt;old&#x20;way&#x20;of&#x20;life&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;as they get older."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Continuity theory</b></span> says people try to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>preserve </i>their <u>old way of life</u></span>&nbsp;as they get older.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Says that our memory is more accurate at recall when we're presented with the same information that was available during encoding.<div>- ""Explains why a subject is able to recall a target word as part of an unrelated word pair at retireval with much more accuracy when prompted with the unrelated word than if presented with a semantically related word that was not available during encoding.""</div></div>"

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Functional&#x20;amnesia&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is where you have significant <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>retro</u></span><u>grade amnesia</u> but <i>no</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">antero</span>grade amnesia - typically brought on by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">physical brain injuries</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Functional amnesia</b></span> is where you have significant <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>retro</u></span><u>grade amnesia</u> but <i>no</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">antero</span>grade amnesia - typically brought on by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">physical brain injuries</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- ""Functional"" in functional amnesia refers to the fact that you can still *function* with this condition, since you can still <u>learn and remember new things</u>.</div>- Difference between dissociative amnesia (most amnesias) and this: dissociative amnesia is both anterograde &amp; retrograde, while this is primarily retrograde. This is also brought on by a brain injruy.</div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Functional amnesia</b></span> is where you have significant <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;retro&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span><u>grade amnesia</u> but <i>no</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""antero"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>grade amnesia - typically brought on by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""physical&#x20;brain&#x20;injuries"" data-ordinal=""2"">[cause]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Functional amnesia</b></span> is where you have significant <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>retro</u></span><u>grade amnesia</u> but <i>no</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">antero</span>grade amnesia - typically brought on by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">physical brain injuries</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- ""Functional"" in functional amnesia refers to the fact that you can still *function* with this condition, since you can still <u>learn and remember new things</u>.</div>- Difference between dissociative amnesia (most amnesias) and this: dissociative amnesia is both anterograde &amp; retrograde, while this is primarily retrograde. This is also brought on by a brain injruy.</div>"
"<div>Difference between most dissociative amnesias &amp; functional amnesia...</div><div><br></div><div>- <b>Dissociative amnesia</b>: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;anterograde&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;retrograde&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[antero/retro/both]</span>, and it's caused by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">major stressors</span>.</div><div>- <b>Functional amnesia</b>: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""only&#x20;&lt;u&gt;retrograde&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[antero/retro/both]</span>, and it's caused by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">physical brain injuries</span>.</div>"	"<div>Difference between most dissociative amnesias &amp; functional amnesia...</div><div><br></div><div>- <b>Dissociative amnesia</b>: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>anterograde</u> &amp; <u>retrograde</u></span>, and it's caused by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">major stressors</span>.</div><div>- <b>Functional amnesia</b>: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">only <u>retrograde</u></span>, and it's caused by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">physical brain injuries</span>.</div>

"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Androcentrism</b></span> is using a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>masculine</i> point of view</span> as the center of one's world.

Then, he receives the pellet after 3 presses of the button.</div>"	"This is an example of a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>variable ratio</b></span> reinforcement schedule:&nbsp;<div><br></div><div>A mouse receives a pellet after 5 presses of a button. Then, he receives the pellet after 3 presses of the button.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The number of responses it takes to get a reward is an inconsistent number that changes between trials.</div><div>- This is the&nbsp;<u>most effective</u>&nbsp;reinforcement schedule.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""what-is-a-variable-ratio-schedule-2796012-5c17efb3c9e77c000183f1f9-1981ef16d418ab7f2b0289b611da2bddebd18e18.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-90637f59e77baa188ad0462f287b5243e0e49d6c.jpg""></div></div>"

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;Women&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[men/women]</span> have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span> <b>hemispheric lateralization</b> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;men&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[men/women]</span>, meaning they have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span> of a significant difference between the functions of each hemisphere." <script>
// Prevent original image from loading before mask
aFade = 50, qFade = 0;
var mask = document.querySelector('#io-overlay>img');
function loaded() {
    var original = document.querySelector('#io-original');
    original.style.visibility = ""visible"";
}
if (mask === null || mask.complete) {
    loaded();
} else {
    mask.addEventListener('load', loaded);
}
</script>

  if (amask.style.display === 'block' || amask.style.display === '')
    amask.style.display = 'none';
  else
    amask.style.display = 'block'
}

<script>
// Prevent original image from loading before mask
aFade = 50, qFade = 0;
var mask = document.querySelector('#io-overlay>img');
function loaded() {
    var original = document.querySelector('#io-original');
    original.style.visibility = ""visible"";
}
if (mask === null || mask.complete) {
    loaded();
} else {
    mask.addEventListener('load', loaded);
}
</script>

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Schizoaffective disorder</b></span> is a disorder where the symptoms of <i>both</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;schizophrenia&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> &amp; a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;mood&#x20;disorder&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x28;mania&#x2F;depression&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> are present <i>simultaneously</i> for at least <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1 month</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Schizoaffective disorder</b></span> is a disorder where the symptoms of <i>both</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>schizophrenia</u></span> &amp; a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>mood disorder</u>&nbsp;(mania/depression)</span> are present <i>simultaneously</i> for at least <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1 month</span>.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Situational poverty</b></span> is a form of poverty where a person's income drops <i>drastically</i> due to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;one&#x20;single&#x20;life&#x20;event&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;natural&#x20;disaster&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> before <i>eventually</i> returning to relative poverty."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Situational poverty</b></span> is a form of poverty where a person's income drops <i>drastically</i> due to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>one single life event</u> (natural disaster)</span> before <i>eventually</i> returning to relative poverty.

- <b>Short-term</b> → memory before 20 seconds.<div>- <b>Long-term</b> → memory longer than 20 seconds.</div></div>"

"Difference between <u>crude birth rate</u> &amp; <u>general fertility rate</u> in population dynamics...<div><br></div><div><b>General fertility rate</b>: total number of live births per <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x2C;000&#x20;child&#x2D;bearing&#x20;aged&#x20;women&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><b>Crude birth rate</b>: total number of live births per <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x2C;000&#x20;women&#x20;&#x28;child&#x2D;bearing&#x20;aged&#x20;or&#x20;not&#x29;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Difference between <u>crude birth rate</u> &amp; <u>general fertility rate</u> in population dynamics...<div><br></div><div><b>General fertility rate</b>: total number of live births per <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1,000 child-bearing aged women.</span></div><div><b>Crude birth rate</b>: total number of live births per <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1,000 women (child-bearing aged or not).</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Crude birth rate (CBR)</b>: number of live births per year per 1,000 individuals.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Total fertility rate (TFR)</b>: average number of children born to a women in her lifetime.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>General fertility rate (GFR)</b>: total number of live births per year for 1,000 child-bearing aged women.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Age-specific fertility rate (ASFR)</b>: number of births per 1,000 women of a specific age-group.</div><b><div><b><br></b></div>Fertility</b>: ability for humans to actually give birth (per 1,000 individuals).<div><b>Fecundity</b>: reproductive&nbsp;<u>potential</u>&nbsp;to have offspring of one single female.</div><div><br></div><div>- This&nbsp;<i><u>is</u></i>&nbsp;factored into population dynamics (fecundity is not).</div></div>"
"Difference between <u>crude birth rate</u> &amp; <u>general fertility rate</u> in population dynamics...<div><br></div><div><b>Crude birth rate</b>: total number of live births per <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x2C;000&#x20;women&#x20;&#x28;child&#x2D;bearing&#x20;aged&#x20;or&#x20;not&#x29;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div><div><b>General fertility rate</b>: total number of live births per <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x2C;000&#x20;child&#x2D;bearing&#x20;aged&#x20;women&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Difference between <u>crude birth rate</u> &amp; <u>general fertility rate</u> in population dynamics...<div><br></div><div><b>Crude birth rate</b>: total number of live births per <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1,000 women (child-bearing aged or not).</span><br></div><div><b>General fertility rate</b>: total number of live births per <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1,000 child-bearing aged women.</span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><u>Both</u> outcomes have their own attractive &amp; unattractive qualities.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-4806efcdefa44eff870ce26a275d02926b2ab4e0.jpg""></div>"

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Mores</b></span> are social norms that are based in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>morality</u></span> &amp; are deemed <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>necessary for society</u></span> - resulting in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;minor&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[minor/major]</span> consequences when they're violated."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Mores</b></span> are social norms that are based in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>morality</u></span> &amp; are deemed <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>necessary for society</u></span> - resulting in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>minor</i></span> consequences when they're violated.

"In the <u>Harlow monkey experiment</u>, monkeys originally paired with ""wire"" mothers <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>were not</i></span> able to regain normal development after <i>later</i> being paired with a cloth mother.

Shows that sometimes the consequences of early neglect are permanent.</div>"

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Negativity bias</b></span> is a cognitive bias where the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>negative aspects</u></span> of a situation are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">focused on</span>, while the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">positive aspects</span> are <i>ignored</i>.

"<div>The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;hippocampus&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[structure]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;amygdala&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[structure]</span> are located in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>temporal</b></span> <b>lobe</b>, which means the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">temporal</span> lobe is <i>crucial</i> for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">memory</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">emotional</span> processing.</div>"	"<div>The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>hippocampus</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>amygdala</u></span> are located in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>temporal</b></span> <b>lobe</b>, which means the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">temporal</span> lobe is <i>crucial</i> for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">memory</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">emotional</span> processing.</div>

"Animals reinforced through <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;fixed&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[fixed/variable]</span> ratio/interval schedules learn the desired behavior <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>faster</i></span> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;variable&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[fixed/variable]</span> ratio/interval schedules." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Subjective</b>: subject discloses how they feel about their own personality traits.</div><div>- <b>Objective</b>: subject's overt behaviors are measured by examiners and their personality is then assessed.</div><div>- <b>Projective</b>: subject is asked to project what they would do/what they would feel in hypothetical scenaiors for examiners to gague their personality (ex: inkblots).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-fa780410e530611638a6d64b8a8e0009d31e38b9.jpg""></div>"

"<div>The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>hippocampus</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>amygdala</u></span> are located in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>temporal</b></span> <b>lobe</b>, which means the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">temporal</span> lobe is <i>crucial</i> for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""memory"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""emotional"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> processing.</div>"	"<div>The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>hippocampus</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>amygdala</u></span> are located in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>temporal</b></span> <b>lobe</b>, which means the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">temporal</span> lobe is <i>crucial</i> for <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">memory</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">emotional</span> processing.</div>

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;General&#x20;paresis&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[disorder]</span> is a disorder characterized by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">psychosis</span> caused by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">late-stage <u>syphilis</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>General paresis</b></span> is a disorder characterized by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">psychosis</span> caused by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">late-stage <u>syphilis</u></span>.

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Delirium&#x20;tremens&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[disorder]</span> is an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>acute</i> episode of delirium</span> caused by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alcohol withdrawls</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Delirium tremens</b></span> is an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>acute</i> episode of delirium</span> caused by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alcohol withdrawls</u></span>.

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Sensory&#x20;coding&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is a <i>generic</i>&nbsp;term that describes the nervous system's ability to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">receive &amp; process sensory information</span>." <hr>

"<b>Incentive theory</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;can&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> be applied to the desire to <u>follow cultural values</u> - not all rewards need to be tangible objects."	"<b>Incentive theory</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>can</i></span> be applied to the desire to <u>follow cultural values</u> - not all rewards need to be tangible objects.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Incentive theory</b>: our behavior is motivated by external factors (including rewards, cultural values, etc.).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-4a14c5beadb37f5c4d4301d5cc8cf2bd8f6f2f31.jpg""></div>"
"Of the personality theories, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>humanistic theory</b></span> is primarily concerned with our <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;internal&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;drive&#x20;towards&#x20;self&#x2D;growth&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"Of the personality theories, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>humanistic theory</b></span> is primarily concerned with our <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u><i>internal</i> drive towards self-growth</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This theory does NOT take external factors into account.</div><div><br></div><img src=""d0e36082c2eb9b7cd13030271707b221.png""></div>"

"Unless you're <i>specifically</i> told a <b>PET scan</b> was used in conjunction with an MRI or CT scan, you should <i>always</i> assume that the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">PET scan <i>cannot</i> detect specific brain regions</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>PET scans</b> <u>do not detect regional brain activity</u>. They can ONLY do that when they're combined with other imaging techniques (MRIs or CT scans).</div><div><br></div><img src=""pet-image.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In <u>expectancy theory</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>expectancy</b></span> component is believing that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""your&#x20;&lt;u&gt;effort&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;will&#x20;&lt;i&gt;allow&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;you&#x20;to&#x20;perform"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>In <u>expectancy theory</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>expectancy</b></span> component is believing that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">your <u>effort</u> will <i>allow</i> you to perform</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Expectancy</b> → effort allows you to perform.</div><div>- <b>Instrumentality</b> → performing will make you feel good.</div><div>- <b>Valence</b> → feeling good.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""79f28d1d9c6c0384fa18f2d23af583ea.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In <u>expectancy theory</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>instrumentality</b></span> component is believing that your <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;performance&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;will&#x20;make&#x20;you&#x20;&lt;i&gt;feel&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;rewarded"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>In <u>expectancy theory</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>instrumentality</b></span> component is believing that your <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>performance</u> will make you <i>feel</i> rewarded</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Expectancy</b>&nbsp;→ effort allows you to perform.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Instrumentality</b>&nbsp;→ performing will make you feel good.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Valence</b>&nbsp;→ feeling good.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""79f28d1d9c6c0384fa18f2d23af583ea.png""></div></div></div>"
"Difference between thinning &amp; fading in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""operant"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> conditioning...<div><br></div><div><b>Thinning</b>: <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reducing the frequency of rewards over time.</span></div><div><b>Fading</b>: <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reducing the frequency of specific prompts over time.</span></div>"	"Difference between thinning &amp; fading in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">operant</span> conditioning...<div><br></div><div><b>Thinning</b>: <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reducing the frequency of rewards over time.</span></div><div><b>Fading</b>: <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reducing the frequency of specific prompts over time.</span></div>

<img src=""content-analysis-process.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Network&#x20;redundency&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is the degree of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>overlapping</i> connections in a <u>social network</u></span> an individual has."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Network redundency</b></span> is the degree of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>overlapping</i> connections in a <u>social network</u></span> an individual has.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Ex: if you're friends with John and Sarah, and they don't know each other, you will have a low network redundency. If John and Sarah know each other, you'll have a high network redundency (both pass similar social information).</div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Network redundency</b></span> is the degree of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;overlapping&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;connections&#x20;in&#x20;a&#x20;&lt;u&gt;social&#x20;network&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> an individual has."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Network redundency</b></span> is the degree of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>overlapping</i> connections in a <u>social network</u></span> an individual has.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Ex: if you're friends with John and Sarah, and they don't know each other, you will have a low network redundency. If John and Sarah know each other, you'll have a high network redundency (both pass similar social information).</div>"

"The processing of&nbsp;<i>novel</i> information happens <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>consciously</i></span> - you <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>must</i> make an effort</span> to process it.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>It's easier to have an idea of the number of times someone walked past you than it is to absorb information from a textbook.</div><div><br></div><div>- Novel information → conscious effort.</div><div>- Frequency of an event happening → automatic.</div><div>- Spatial information → automatic.</div><div>- Tempral (time) information → automatic.</div></div>"

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Exchange mobility</b></span> says that for the number of people that move <i>beyond</i>&nbsp;the middle class, there is an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;equal&#x20;number&#x20;of&#x20;people&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;that&#x20;&lt;i&gt;fall&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;i&gt;down&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> into the middle class - the numbers <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""remain&#x20;constant"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Exchange mobility</b></span> says that for the number of people that move <i>beyond</i>&nbsp;the middle class, there is an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equal number of people</u> that <i>fall</i> <i>down</i></span> into the middle class - the numbers <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">remain constant</span>.

"<div>In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""neurotic"" data-ordinal=""3"">[category]</span> defense mechanisms, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>rationalization</b></span> is when you <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>make excuses</u>&nbsp;for your behaviors</span> in order to cope.</div>"	"<div>In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">neurotic</span> defense mechanisms, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>rationalization</b></span> is when you <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>make excuses</u>&nbsp;for your behaviors</span> in order to cope.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Person with an eating disorder: ""But it's okay because I only skipped breakfast &amp; lunch because I planned on having a big dinner!""</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ddf77e4808f5a220c384f0f01eeaf32f.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In <u>social constructionism</u>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>institutional facts</b></span> are facts that <i>are </i>social constructs but <i>do </i>ultimately <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""depend&#x20;on&#x20;other&#x20;facts"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, like money &amp; the paper it's printed on."	"In <u>social constructionism</u>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>institutional facts</b></span> are facts that <i>are </i>social constructs but <i>do </i>ultimately <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">depend on other facts</span>, like money &amp; the paper it's printed on.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Brute facts</b>: most basic phenomenon that governs existence (atomic theory) that <u>absolutely</u> do exist.</div><div><b>Institutional facts</b>: facts that rely on other facts (we socially assign value to money, but it still depends on the paper it's printed on).</div><div><br></div><img src=""6cf2cb8319ade53b5c8574c35d9e92b9.png""></div>"
"<div>Our <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>social capital</b></span> encompasses the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;tangible&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;benefits&#x20;we&#x20;receive&#x20;from&#x20;our&#x20;&lt;u&gt;social&#x20;networks&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>Our <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>social capital</b></span> encompasses the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>tangible</i> benefits we receive from our <u>social networks</u></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: because you and your neighbor get along, you two share tools with each other (not afraid to ask for a favor).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-3b40de4dbd37983c15c3ad8a736ecc41c81d641c.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""670px-tell-the-difference-between-borrow-and-lend-step-2.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""3-Dimensions-of-Social-Capital.png""></div>"
"What's the <i>difference</i> between the <u>actor-observer</u> bias &amp; the <u>self-serving</u> bias?<div><br></div><div><b>Actor-observer</b>: focuses on <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""both&#x20;our&#x20;&lt;i&gt;own&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;behaviors&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;&lt;i&gt;other&#x27;s&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;behaviors"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><b>Self-serving</b>: focuses on <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""only&#x20;our&#x20;own&#x20;behaviors"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"What's the <i>difference</i> between the <u>actor-observer</u> bias &amp; the <u>self-serving</u> bias?<div><br></div><div><b>Actor-observer</b>: focuses on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">both our <i>own</i> behaviors &amp; <i>other's</i> behaviors</span>.</div><div><b>Self-serving</b>: focuses on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">only our own behaviors</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <u>Actor-observer</u> → we tend to blame our failures on situational things, but attribute our successes to dispositional things. We might also blame others' successes on situational factors but blame their failures on their internal factors (dispositional).</div><div>- <u>Self-serving bias</u> → we blame our failures on other things and our successes on internal factors.</div><div><br></div><img src=""de19db609180f2ff76fc386290a9ee1c.png""></div>"
"What's the <i>difference</i> between the <u>actor-observer</u> bias &amp; the <u>self-serving</u> bias?<div><br></div><div><b>Self-serving</b>: focuses on <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""only&#x20;our&#x20;own&#x20;behaviors"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><b>Actor-observer</b>: focuses on <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""both&#x20;our&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;i&gt;own&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;behaviors&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;i&gt;other&#x27;s&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;behaviors"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"What's the <i>difference</i> between the <u>actor-observer</u> bias &amp; the <u>self-serving</u> bias?<div><br></div><div><b>Self-serving</b>: focuses on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">only our own behaviors</span>.</div><div><b>Actor-observer</b>: focuses on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">both our&nbsp;<i>own</i>&nbsp;behaviors &amp;&nbsp;<i>other's</i>&nbsp;behaviors</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <u>Actor-observer</u> → we tend to blame our failures on situational things, but attribute our successes to dispositional things. We might also blame others' successes on situational factors but blame their failures on their internal factors (dispositional).</div><div>- <u>Self-serving bias</u> → we blame our failures on other things and our successes on internal factors.</div><div><br></div><img src=""de19db609180f2ff76fc386290a9ee1c.png""></div>"
"In <b>drive-reduction theory</b>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>drive</u></span> state is the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;internal&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;state&#x20;of&#x20;tension"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"In <b>drive-reduction theory</b>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>drive</u></span> state is the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>internal</i>&nbsp;state of tension</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- We are <b><u>driven</u></b> to fulfill our need.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""cf4f034872b9ff166892ea58d555b66a.png""><br></div></div>"
"The stages of <b>drive-reduction theory</b>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Need"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""drive"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""action"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"The stages of <b>drive-reduction theory</b>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Need</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">drive</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">action</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <u>Need</u>: thing that fuels the drive.</div><div>- <u>Drive</u>: internal state of tension/conflict.</div><div>- <u>Action</u>: behavior you do to resolve the tension.</div><div><br></div><img src=""cf4f034872b9ff166892ea58d555b66a.png""></div>"
"In <u>drive-reduction theory</u>, the stage that <i>immedietly preceeds</i> the <b>action</b> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""drive"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> state."	"In <u>drive-reduction theory</u>, the stage that <i>immedietly preceeds</i> the <b>action</b> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">drive</span> state.

"In order for the <b>serial position effect</b> to take place, the recall task must be given <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;immedietly&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;after&#x20;presenting&#x20;the&#x20;words"" data-ordinal=""1"">[when?]</span>."	"In order for the <b>serial position effect</b> to take place, the recall task must be given <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>immedietly</u> after presenting the words</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Waiting after presenting the list of words would result in a <u>disappearance</u> of the <b>recency effect</b> (since the recency effect depends on short-term memory).</div><div><br></div><img src=""978-0-387-79948-3_19_Part_Fig1-2232_HTML.gif""></div>"
"If researchers studying the <b>serial position effect</b> <i>waited</i> 20 minutes to administer the recall task after presenting the words, they'd see a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;disappearance&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;u&gt;recency&#x20;effect&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, since that relies <i>entirely</i> on <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""short&#x2D;term&#x20;memory"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"If researchers studying the <b>serial position effect</b> <i>waited</i> 20 minutes to administer the recall task after presenting the words, they'd see a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>disappearance</i> of the <u>recency effect</u></span>, since that relies <i>entirely</i> on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">short-term memory</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is why the recall tasks are typically given <u>immedietly</u> after the words are presented.</div><div>- Below shows the normal serial position effect (not under the conditions described in the question).</div><div><br></div><img src=""978-0-387-79948-3_19_Part_Fig1-2232_HTML.gif""></div>"

"In conditioning, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>discriminating stimulus</b></span> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;cue&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> that's presented to the participant."	"In conditioning, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>discriminating stimulus</b></span> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>cue</u></span> that's presented to the participant.
